messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing psychological case vignettes 
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

For each patient you receive, you will be given:
- Their demographics
- Their self-reported symptoms per PTSD component
- Weighted causal connections between components (the active cognitive graph)
- Forbidden causal connections that must never appear

Your job is to construct a realistic, fully personalised clinical portrait using only 
the information provided.

Core constraints that apply to every vignette:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not present in the active graph.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", 
  "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". 
  Both components may appear — just never connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief 
  or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y"). Describe the 
  avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground every clinical detail in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate 
  symptoms or infer unlisted ones.
- Use self-reported items as the basis for concrete clinical illustrations. Each component 
  should appear as lived experience, not as a restated label. A reported trigger should 
  appear as a specific moment in the patient's life; a memory quality should be shown 
  through what the patient says or does, not named directly.
- The traumatic event account should make clear why this patient's specific reported 
  triggers are potent — the event narrative and the trigger list must feel causally coherent.
- The causal connections are weighted (0 to 1). Stronger connections (weight > 0.6) should 
  be narratively prominent — more detail, appearing earlier, forming the dominant maintaining 
  cycle. Weaker connections (weight < 0.3) should appear briefly or in passing.
- Some components appear in the graph but with all connections weighted at 0.
  These are fully forbidden from any causal connection. Describe them in a
  dedicated sentence or paragraph that stands alone — do not place them in the
  same sentence as any other component, and do not use transitional language
  between them and surrounding content that implies sequence or response
  (e.g. "then", "so", "as a result", "this means", "which leads to", "in response").
- The patient's occupation and daily environment must appear as the specific setting in which 
  at least one trigger or avoidance behaviour is concretely encountered — not merely mentioned 
  as background.
- Give the patient a realistic first name consistent with their ethnicity and gender, and refer to them by name throughout the vignette.

Output format — write 300–450 words in third person, covering in this order:
1. Presenting complaints and brief trauma account — what happened, the moment of peak danger, 
   and what the patient did or failed to do. The event must make clear why this patient's 
   specific triggers are so potent.
2. Memory phenomenology — do not restate memory qualities as labels. Render them as clinical 
   observation: show what the patient says or does that reveals how the memory is held 
   (e.g. instead of "his memory is held as snapshots", write "he describes being pulled back 
   suddenly to a single frozen image of...").
3. The patient's central stuck point — the specific belief about themselves, others, or the 
   world that the trauma confirmed or created, derived from their Negative Appraisals items. 
   Render this in close-paraphrase of the patient's own voice.
4. Threat monitoring and avoidance behaviours — illustrated as concrete moments drawn from 
   the patient's reported items, not as category labels.
5. Maintaining factors — show how the dominant weighted connections (weight > 0.6) form a 
   self-reinforcing cycle. Include at least one specific behavioural example of relational 
   or occupational impact drawn from the patient's demographic context.

Write for a clinical case conference audience. Avoid excessive jargon.

Your previous vignette failed validation. You must rewrite it to remove the flagged causal connections.

Previous vignette:
Mai is a 59-year-old South Eastern Asian woman who remains in a relationship and works as a combat pilot. She presents with persistent PTSD symptoms after torture, describing a period in which she was held, controlled, and exposed to repeated physical pain. She remembers the moment of peak danger as one in which she had no clear way to predict what would happen next and no protection to rely on. In daily life, a specific temperature on her skin can make her go rigid, the anniversary of the event is difficult, and being alone with women leaves her feeling uneasy and on guard.

When Mai talks about the trauma, her account narrows to a single bodily impression rather than unfolding as a full story. She speaks of the feel of the air and contact against her skin, and she returns to that detail in a way that interrupts everything else she is trying to say. At times she seems pulled into one frozen scene, with little sense of the wider sequence around it.

Mai says the event left her with the conviction that she “can’t deal with even the slightest upset.” She also says that the people and institutions who should have protected her let her down, and that she feels guilty for surviving when others did not. Her voice is steady, but the shame is clear when she says these things.

She watches for danger in her body and in the room. A cool draft on her skin, the approach of the anniversary, or finding herself alone with women makes her tense and scan her surroundings. At work as a combat pilot, she notices herself becoming guarded in the cockpit and more distant with colleagues during the anniversary period. She avoids unnecessary time alone with female crew members.

The remembered torture, her sense of present threat, and her appraisals sit close together in her daily life. The skin sensation, the anniversary, and being alone with women are all moments that she experiences as loaded with danger. The trauma memory remains vivid, and her fear is never far away. Her beliefs about not being able to cope, being failed by those who should have protected her, and surviving when others did not are spoken alongside the same triggers and the same sense of alarm. She also uses strategies that keep her braced and watchful, and those habits leave the memory close at hand.

Violations to fix:
- Triggers → Threat: The vignette explicitly links triggers with a sense of danger/threat: the sentence says the skin sensation, anniversary, and being alone with women are moments she experiences as loaded with danger. This names triggers and threat in the same sentence with causal/associative language, so the forbidden edge is violated.
  quote: ""The skin sensation, the anniversary, and being alone with women are all moments that she experiences as loaded with danger.""
- Negative Appraisals → Threat: Her negative appraisals are explicitly paired with alarm/danger in the same sentence: her beliefs are spoken alongside the same triggers and the same sense of alarm. This directly links appraisals to threat language in one sentence.
  quote: ""Her beliefs about not being able to cope, being failed by those who should have protected her, and surviving when others did not are spoken alongside the same triggers and the same sense of alarm.""
- Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies: The vignette says she uses strategies that keep her braced and watchful, and these are presented in the same sentence as her appraisals. However, the sentence does not state that the appraisals cause the strategies; it only co-occurs, so this forbidden edge is absent.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the negative appraisals cause the strategies."
- Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies: Triggers and strategies are both mentioned, but no single sentence states that the triggers cause the strategies. The text says she uses strategies and separately lists triggers, without explicit causal language between them.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the triggers cause the strategies."
- Triggers → Memory: The vignette mentions trauma memory and triggers, but no single sentence states that triggers cause the memory. The closest sentence links the skin sensation, anniversary, and being alone with women to danger, not to memory.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the triggers cause the memory."
- Negative Appraisals → Memory: The vignette mentions her appraisals and the trauma memory, but no single sentence explicitly says the appraisals cause the memory. They are described together without causal language.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the negative appraisals cause the memory."
- Memory → Maladaptive Strategies: Memory and strategies are both present, but the text does not explicitly say the memory causes the strategies. It says she uses strategies and that the memory remains close at hand, which is not a causal statement.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the memory causes the strategies."
- Memory → Threat: The vignette does not explicitly state that memory causes threat. It says the trauma memory remains vivid and her fear is never far away, but that is co-occurrence rather than a causal link.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the memory causes the threat."
- Memory → Negative Appraisals: The vignette contains memory and appraisals, but no sentence says the memory causes the appraisals. They are mentioned separately.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the memory causes the negative appraisals."
- Memory → Triggers: The vignette includes memory and triggers, but does not explicitly say the memory causes the triggers. There is no sentence with both terms connected by causal language.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the memory causes the triggers."
- Threat → Maladaptive Strategies: Threat and strategies both appear, but the text does not explicitly state that threat causes the strategies. It says she uses strategies that keep her braced and watchful, without tying them causally to threat.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the threat causes the strategies."
- Threat → Memory: Threat and memory are both present, but no sentence states that threat causes memory. The vignette discusses them separately.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the threat causes the memory."
- Threat → Negative Appraisals: Threat and appraisals are mentioned together, but no sentence explicitly says threat causes the appraisals. The relevant sentence says the appraisals are spoken alongside the same sense of alarm, which is not directional causality.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the threat causes the negative appraisals."
- Threat → Triggers: Threat and triggers co-occur in the vignette, but no sentence states that threat causes the triggers. The sentence about danger describes the triggers as being experienced as loaded with danger, not danger producing the triggers.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the threat causes the triggers."
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: The vignette says strategies leave the memory close at hand, but it does not explicitly say the strategies cause the memory in a single sentence with clear causal language between the two components. The phrasing is closer to a consequence description, but not enough to establish the forbidden edge under the strict rule set.
  quote: ""She also uses strategies that keep her braced and watchful, and those habits leave the memory close at hand.""
- Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals: Strategies and appraisals appear together in the final sentence, but there is no explicit causal wording showing strategies cause appraisals. The sentence mentions both, but the causal direction is not stated.
  quote: "No single sentence states that the strategies cause the negative appraisals."

How to fix violations:
- A violation exists only when EXPLICIT causal language directly connects a forbidden pair — words like "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by".
- Narrative proximity is NOT a violation. Two components can appear in the same paragraph or even the same sentence without implying causation, as long as no explicit causal link is stated.
- To remove a violation: delete or rephrase the explicit causal language. You do NOT need to remove the components themselves — just decouple them.
- You may restructure entire paragraphs if needed. Do not aim for minimal edits if the structure itself is the problem.
- Do not introduce any new explicit causal links that are not in the required edge list.

Mai is a 59-year-old South Eastern Asian woman who remains in a relationship and works as a combat pilot. She presents with persistent PTSD symptoms after torture, describing a period in which she was held, controlled, and exposed to repeated physical pain. She remembers the moment of peak danger as one in which she had no clear way to predict what would happen next and no protection to rely on. In daily life, a specific temperature on her skin can make her go rigid, the anniversary of the event is difficult, and being alone with women leaves her uneasy and watchful.

When Mai talks about the trauma, her account narrows to a single bodily impression rather than unfolding as a full story. She speaks of the feel of the air and contact against her skin, and she returns to that detail in a way that interrupts everything else she is trying to say. At times she seems pulled into one frozen scene, with little sense of the wider sequence around it.

Mai says the event left her with the conviction that she “can’t deal with even the slightest upset.” She also says that the people and institutions who should have protected her let her down, and that she feels guilty for surviving when others did not. Her voice is steady, but the shame is clear when she says these things.

She watches for danger in her body and in the room. A cool draft on her skin, the approach of the anniversary, or finding herself alone with women makes her tense and scan her surroundings. At work as a combat pilot, she notices herself becoming guarded in the cockpit and more distant with colleagues during the anniversary period. She avoids unnecessary time alone with female crew members.

Mai keeps the trauma memory, the sense of threat, and her harsh self-appraisal in close proximity in daily life, but she does not describe them as separate experiences. Her body stays primed for danger, her attention catches on the same bodily sensations and calendar dates, and her mind returns to the torture scene in fragments. She speaks about failing to cope, about being let down by the people and institutions that should have protected her, and about surviving when others did not in the same breath as those remembered moments. In the cockpit, and in social space at work, she remains guarded and keeps distance from female colleagues, especially around the anniversary.

output:

She presents with persistent PTSD symptoms after torture, describing a period in which she was held, controlled, and exposed to repeated physical pain. She remembers the moment of peak danger as one in which she had no clear way to predict what would happen next and no protection to rely on. In daily life, a specific temperature on her skin can make her go rigid, the anniversary of the event is difficult, and being alone with women leaves her uneasy and watchful.

When Mai talks about the trauma, her account narrows to a single bodily impression rather than unfolding as a full story. She speaks of the feel of the air and contact against her skin, and she returns to that detail in a way that interrupts everything else she is trying to say. At times she seems pulled into one frozen scene, with little sense of the wider sequence around it.

Mai says the event left her with the conviction that she “can’t deal with even the slightest upset.” She also says that the people and institutions who should have protected her let her down, and that she feels guilty for surviving when others did not. Her voice is steady, but the shame is clear when she says these things.

She watches for danger in her body and in the room. A cool draft on her skin, the approach of the anniversary, or finding herself alone with women makes her tense and scan her surroundings. At work as a combat pilot, she notices herself becoming guarded in the cockpit and more distant with colleagues during the anniversary period. She avoids unnecessary time alone with female crew members.

Mai keeps the trauma memory, the sense of threat, and her harsh self-appraisal in close proximity in daily life, but she does not describe them as separate experiences. Her body stays primed for danger, her attention catches on the same bodily sensations and calendar dates, and her mind returns to the torture scene in fragments. She speaks about failing to cope, about being let down by the people and institutions that should have protected her, and about surviving when others did not in the same breath as those remembered moments. In the cockpit, and in social space at work, she remains guarded and keeps distance from female colleagues, especially around the anniversary.
